questions.question: Why is multicast more efficient than unicast for a live stream with many viewers?
options: Multicast sends one copy from the source; the network replicates only at branches where receivers joined, so source egress stays constant
Multicast uses TCP which is faster
questions.explanation: With unicast the source sends one copy per viewer, so egress scales linearly with audience size. Multicast sends one copy to a group address; routers replicate only where at least one downstream receiver has joined via IGMP.

questions.question: How many IPv4 multicast group addresses map to a single Ethernet MAC address, and why?
options: 32 IP groups share one MAC because only the low 23 bits of the IP group are copied into the MAC
All class D addresses share one MAC
questions.explanation: The Ethernet multicast MAC uses only the low 23 bits of the IP group address, dropping bit 24 of the third octet. That 5-bit loss means 2^5 = 32 IP groups map to each MAC. The IP stack filters by full IP address to resolve the ambiguity.

questions.question: What does an IGMP Leave Group message (v2/v3) let the router do that IGMPv1 could not?
options: Prune the group immediately instead of waiting for the query timeout to expire
questions.explanation: IGMPv1 had no leave message, so a router kept forwarding a group until the query interval timed out with no response. IGMPv2/v3 adds a Leave Group message so the router can send a group-specific query and prune the interface right away, reclaiming bandwidth.

questions.question: What problem does IGMP snooping solve on a layer-2 switch?
options: It inspects IGMP reports and leaves to learn which ports joined which groups, so the switch forwards multicast only to those ports instead of flooding
questions.explanation: A switch does not route IP, so by default it floods multicast to every port. IGMP snooping has the switch inspect IGMP control messages to build a group-to-port map, forwarding frames only to ports that joined the group, avoiding waste.
